title: Lesson 1: Loving Your Neighbors
description: The second great commandment is not a separate assignment from the first. It is the overflow of it. When the Father's love lands in your heart, it does not stay there. It moves outward toward the people God has placed near you. In this lesson the Good Samaritan shows you what neighbor-love really looks like, and the Greatest Commandment diagram from Lesson 1 of Growing Deep returns to remind you where that love comes from in the first place.

blocks.text: Welcome to Growing Up

The 200-level path has three connected courses: Growing Deep (201), Growing Up (202), and Growing Out (203). In Starting (101) the roots went down into the soil of the gospel. In Growing Deep your identity in Christ took shape and the foundations of life with God were planted. Now in Growing Up, the branches start to extend outward. These ten lessons are about what happens when the love that went deep in you begins to reach the people around you, your neighbors, your family, your church, and the people God has placed in your daily path.

blocks.alt: Growing Together Discipleship Series: Growing Up in Discipleship highlighted.

blocks.text: In the first lesson of Growing Deep you explored what it means to love God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength. That was the vertical beam of the cross, God reaching down to you and your love going back up to Him. This first lesson of Growing Up is about the horizontal beam, the place where that love overflows toward other people. Loving your neighbor is not a separate command added on top of loving God. It is what loving God looks like when it starts to move.

blocks.alt: A Samaritan kneeling beside a wounded man on a desolate road

blocks.title: The Good Samaritan
blocks.text:
A man was traveling the road from Jerusalem to Jericho, seventeen miles of wilderness, steep and winding, known for bandits. He never made it. He was attacked, stripped, beaten, and left for dead on the side of the road.

A priest came by first. He saw the man and crossed to the other side. Then a Levite, a temple worker, passed the same way. He looked, and he crossed to the other side too. These were not evil men. They had important religious work waiting for them. But they were not moved by suffering close at hand where they would have to get their own hands dirty.

Then a Samaritan came. To understand what this meant, you have to know that Jews and Samaritans had despised each other for centuries. The hostility between them was deep and personal, ethnic, religious, and political all at once. A Jewish audience hearing this story would have expected the Samaritan to be the villain, not the hero. But Jesus made him the hero.

The Samaritan stopped. He knelt in the dirt beside a stranger who, by every social and cultural standard, was his enemy. He poured oil and wine on the wounds, lifted the man onto his own donkey, carried him to an inn, and paid for his care out of his own pocket. He did not ask the man's name, his nationality, or his theology. He saw a human being broken on the ground, and he moved toward the mess.

"Which of these three, do you think, proved to be a neighbor to the man who fell among the robbers?" (Luke 10:36)

Sit with the picture for a moment before any teaching tries to make sense of it. Picture the road, the wounded man, the two religious figures stepping to the far side, and then the one who was supposed to be the enemy kneeling down to bind up the wounds. The meaning underneath the image is going to take the rest of this lesson to unfold, and it is going to begin somewhere unexpected.

1.1 - As you sit with the picture of the Samaritan kneeling in the dirt beside someone who, by every cultural standard, was his enemy, what stirs in you? Honestly, which of the three travelers do you most resemble in your daily life right now?
Where Does the Love Come From?

Before asking who your neighbor is, there is a more important question to settle. Where does the love come from? If the answer is willpower, you will run dry. If the answer is guilt, you will burn out. If the answer is religious duty, you will quietly start keeping a tally of who deserves your kindness and who does not. The whole second commandment depends on a source you cannot generate yourself.
But God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

The love comes from God. He moved first, coming toward you in sacrifice and restoration while you were still His enemy. He did not wait for you to clean up, earn it, or deserve it. He just came. And because He moved toward you, you now have something real to give to others. You are not generating love from an empty tank. You are giving away what has already been poured into you.
We love because he first loved us.

That little verse holds the whole engine of the second commandment. Love does not start with you trying harder. Love starts with you receiving. The rhythm Lesson 1 of Growing Deep named was simple: receive, then respond. Receive the Father's love, then respond by loving Him back and loving the people He has placed near you. John Piper once defined love this way: love is the overflow of joy in God that gladly meets the needs of others. That is the kind of love Jesus is asking for, not the kind that grinds through service to earn approval, and not the kind that forces itself to care about people out of guilt. It is love that flows freely from a heart already satisfied in God. The source never changes. Only the direction shifts, from vertical to horizontal.
The Second Is Like the First

And he said to him, "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind. This is the great and first commandment. And a second is like it: You shall love your neighbor as yourself."

Notice that Jesus calls the second commandment like the first. Not lesser than it. Like it. The two are the same kind of love, just pointed in different directions. The world around you sees only the horizontal. People care for each other, sacrifice for each other, and build community all the time without ever turning to God. As a follower of Jesus, you bring the vertical love into those horizontal spaces. When you love your neighbor, you are not just being a good person. You are putting the character of God on display in a place where no one was looking for Him.

And look at the phrase as yourself. Jesus is not shaming you for caring about your own needs. You already think about your meals, your safety, your comfort, and your future. That kind of self-concern is built into being human. Jesus takes that natural concern and redirects it. As often as you think about your own well-being, think about the well-being of the person next to you. As you work for a healthy family and home, want that for the family across the street. As you long to feel safe and secure, seek that for the people around you. Your own self-concern becomes the measuring stick for how much attention you give to the people God has placed in your life.

The diagram below is the same one you walked through in the first lesson of Growing Deep. The shape has not changed. The cross is still the cross. But the focus is going to shift. In 201 you traced the love coming down from the Father, landing in your heart, and rising back to Him in worship and obedience. In this lesson, watch what happens at the foot of the cross when that love does not stop. Tap "Begin" and walk through it again, this time with your eyes on the horizontal beam.

This is why the Greatest Commandment is shaped like a cross. The vertical beam is the love between God and you, His coming down to you and your love rising back to Him. The horizontal beam is where that love overflows into the people God has placed around you. You cannot get to the horizontal without first receiving the vertical, which means the whole shape of the Christian life starts not with what you do for God, but with what He has already done for you. Loving your neighbor is what the vertical love looks like when it spills out sideways.

1.2 - Where do you tend to live as if you have to make this love yourself, by sheer effort or willpower? What would change in your week if you started from "I have already been loved" instead of "I have to love better"?
Who Is My Neighbor?

Now that the source question is settled, the scope question can finally be asked honestly. The Samaritan story was Jesus' answer to a man who was trying to dodge the answer. The man was a religious lawyer, an expert in the Law of Moses. He had just heard Jesus quote the two Greatest Commandments and he agreed with both. Then he asked the question that gave him away: "And who is my neighbor?"

Luke gives you the reason behind the question. The lawyer wanted to justify himself. He was not asking because he was trying to expand his love. He was asking because he was trying to limit it. He wanted Jesus to draw a small circle around the people he was responsible for so he could safely ignore everyone outside that circle. Most people have asked some version of his question at some point. Who actually counts? Who is on my list? Who can I leave off?

Jesus answered with a story that broke the circle apart. A priest and a Levite, the most religious figures in the culture, walked past a wounded man on the side of the road. A Samaritan, the very last person a Jewish audience expected to see in the role of hero, stopped and gave the man everything he had. And then Jesus did something the lawyer was not ready for. He flipped the question. The lawyer had asked, "Who is my neighbor?" meaning, "Which people qualify for my love?" Jesus asked back, "Which of these three proved to be a neighbor to the man?" In one sentence, Jesus changed the question from What kind of person is my neighbor? to What kind of person am I becoming?

That is the part of the parable that still confronts every believer. It is easy to have a heart for distant causes while ignoring the mess right in front of you. The priest and the Levite probably had ministry plans that afternoon, places to be, important work to do for God. But love for your neighbor is not an emotion you feel from a distance. It is a decision you make when it costs you something close at hand.

1.3 - Is there anyone in your life you have been quietly keeping outside your circle of concern? Someone different from you, hard to love, easy to overlook, or culturally and politically on the other side of a line? What would it look like to move toward them this week instead of crossing to the other side?
Love That Costs Something

By this we know love, that he laid down his life for us, and we ought to lay down our lives for the brothers. But if anyone has the world's goods and sees his brother in need, yet closes his heart against him, how does God's love abide in him? Little children, let us not love in word or talk but in deed and in truth.

This is a sobering passage, but it is not meant to crush you. It is meant to give you clarity. Love is not a feeling you wait to feel. It is a decision you make with your hands, your time, your money, and your attention. "In deed and in truth" means love has to land somewhere. It has to show up in a real life, on a real day, for a real person. The Samaritan used his own oil and wine, his own donkey, and his own money. The cost of the rescue fell entirely on him. Love that costs you nothing is not the kind of love Jesus is asking for, because the love that came down from the Father at the cross cost Him everything.

Take a breath here, because the gospel walks with you in this verse. You do not love your neighbor in order to become a child of God. You love your neighbor because you already are one. A child does not earn a place in the family by being kind. The child is born into it, and kindness becomes the family resemblance. Your love for others is not what earns your salvation. It is what proves it is real, because the same Spirit who united you to the Father is the Spirit who is forming the Father's own kindness in you.

Practical love begins with small ordinary things. Learn the name of the neighbor across the street. Put the phone down when a friend is talking. Bring a meal to a family going through a hard season. Ask the coworker who talks too much one real question, then stay long enough to hear the real answer. Notice the person at the checkout who looks like they are barely holding it together, and let your eyes meet theirs. None of these acts will make a headline, but they are the kind of love that makes someone ask why you are different. And that question opens a door no argument ever could.

1.4 - Walk through your normal week in your mind, your street, your job, your regular stops. Who has God placed in your path that you have been treating more like an interruption than an assignment? Write down one or two names.
This Week's Practice

Pick one of the names you just wrote down and do one costly thing for that person this week. Costly does not have to mean expensive. Costly means it costs you something in attention, time, or comfort. Learn their name if you do not know it. Ask a real question and stay long enough to hear the real answer. Bring them something they did not ask for. The goal is not to check a box. The goal is to let the love that the Father has poured into you spill over toward one specific person this week, and to pay attention to what God does in you when you move toward someone instead of past them.

Also this week, read Luke 10:25-37 slowly, the full Good Samaritan passage. As you read, sit with the question Jesus asked at the end of the parable: "Which of these three proved to be a neighbor?" Let the question settle, and ask honestly which character in the story you most resemble right now.
Lesson 1 Review

Loving your neighbor is not a separate assignment from loving God. It is the overflow of it. The love comes down from the Father, lands in your heart, and moves outward toward the people God has placed in your path. You do not love your neighbor to earn your way home. You love because you have already been brought home. The vertical produces the horizontal, and the horizontal is where the world around you finally sees the love of God on display.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson?
What is one step you are going to take this week?
What is a prayer request for this week?
